retrieved_docs.title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol is a class of lipoproteins characterized by a dense protein structure and a diameter of approximately 8–12 nanometers. [1] HDL particles are composed of a hydrophobic core consisting largely of esters and triglycerides, surrounded by a layer of phospholipids, free cholesterol, and apolipoproteins. HDL levels are routinely measured via enzymatic assays, which quantify the cholesterol content of HDL particles after the precipitation or inhibition of other lipoprotein classes. Additionally, different methods have been used to subclassify HDL particle number: size and density via ultracentrifugal flotation rate and mass concentration of lipoprotein particles. [2] Further, HDL can be subcategorized by gel electrophoresis and by apolipoprotein composition.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipoprotein Metabolism -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: Lipoproteins are complex molecules that involve several different components. They contain a central core made of triglycerides and cholesterol esters. [1] Fatty acids that are cleaved from triglycerides can be used for energy storage or production, and cholesterol is critical for steroid synthesis, cellular membrane formation, and bile acids. Surrounding this core is a mix of phospholipids, free cholesterol, and apolipoproteins (apo). The apolipoproteins are particularly important, as they play a role in classifying the lipoprotein into one of five main classes: chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), low-density lipoprotein (LDL), and high-density lipoprotein (HDL) provide structure and function in lipid metabolism. The differentiation between these classes depends on the size of the molecule, its lipid content, and the type of apolipoprotein it features. HDL, colloquially known as “good cholesterol,” participates in reverse cholesterol transport, while LDL, colloquially known as “bad cholesterol,” promotes atherosclerosis.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, High Density Lipoprotein -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lipoproteins are complex particles that transport lipids, such as phospholipids, triglycerides, and cholesterol, between cells. There are 5 different types of lipoproteins, which are classified according to their density and composition: chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), low-density lipoprotein (LDL), and high-density lipoprotein (HDL). As per its denomination, HDL has the highest density of lipoproteins and the highest proportion of proteins to lipids. [1] HDL is particularly interesting in medicine, as research has shown a strong inverse association between HDL cholesterol concentration and the risk of atherosclerosis (see Image. Lipoprotein Metabolism). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: The composition of VLDL consists of approximately 90% lipids and 10% proteins. This proportion gives VLDL a low density of 0.96 to 1.006 g/ml, hence its name. Of its total lipid component, triglycerides take up to 70% of VLDL’s mass, with cholesterol esters and fatty acids making up the rest. [5] Regarding its lower protein component, VLDL is associated with multiple apolipoproteins: Apo B-100, Apo C-I, Apo C-II, Apo C-III, and Apo E. [6] Apolipoproteins are cofactors for numerous enzymes throughout the body, ligands for surface receptors, and essential structural elements. The biological functions and metabolism of VLDL are strongly related to these apolipoproteins, as well as VLDL’s potential role in disease development. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: High-density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol plays a crucial role in cardiovascular health due to its antiatherogenic, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. HDL cholesterol is involved in reverse cholesterol transport, a process by which excess cholesterol is removed from peripheral tissues and delivered to the liver for excretion. Composed of a dense protein structure with a core of esters and triglycerides, HDL particles are further surrounded by phospholipids, free cholesterol, and apolipoproteins. HDL levels are typically quantified through enzymatic assays, and various methods such as ultracentrifugation, electrophoresis, and apolipoprotein profiling are used to classify HDL subtypes. Although historically considered protective, recent research results suggest that HDL function and quality may be more clinically relevant than absolute quantity. Low HDL cholesterol is linked to increased cardiovascular risk, particularly in patients with underlying conditions such as metabolic syndrome, obesity, insulin resistance, and tobacco use. Current therapies primarily target low-density lipoprotein cholesterol, leaving a clinical gap in the effective management of HDL-related risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Low Density Lipoprotein -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Both cholesterol esters (CE) and triacylglycerols (TG) are insoluble in water (plasma); hence they are packaged as lipoproteins comprising an inner core of CE and TG with a surface coat of apolipoproteins, free cholesterol (FC), and phospholipids. Packaged as lipoproteins, they can be transported to various tissues where needed. By differing the lipid and protein concentrations, up to 5 different lipoproteins can be produced, such as chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). LDL is the predominant carrier of serum cholesterol (around 67%) and delivers it to tissues of need such as the adrenal glands, gonads, and other tissues. With a density of 1.019 to 1.063 g/ml, LDL contains 20% protein and 50% cholesterol (CE and FC) and displays beta mobility on electrophoresis. This review focuses on LDL, especially its biochemistry, measurement, and clinical significance. [1] [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Low HDL Cholesterol -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Fasting lipid panels will reveal the levels of total cholesterol, low-density lipoproteins, HDL, and triglycerides. Additionally, nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) can measure particle numbers. However, quantification via NMR is not standardized and is often difficult to interpret. [2] [20] Additionally, certain diseases (eg, Fish Eye disease) are associated with proteinuria and nephropathy; assessing kidney function with a basic metabolic panel and urinalysis is reasonable if this disease is suspected. Furthermore, blood sugar quantification is reasonable if HDL abnormalities associated with metabolic syndrome are a consideration.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, High Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: HDL synthesis occurs in the liver and the intestines. The first step of HDL synthesis involves the production of its main structural apolipoproteins, Apo-AI. Apo-AI is the structural protein of HDL and receives cholesterol and phospholipids from enterocytes and hepatocytes via transporter ABCA1, forming pre-beta HDL. HDL travels in circulation and receives free cholesterol and phospholipids from peripheral tissues, chylomicrons, and VLDL. As mentioned, Apo-AI acts as a cofactor for LCAT. LCAT converts free cholesterol at the surface of HDL to cholesterol esters, which are then incorporated into the cholesterol ester core of HDL. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Dyslipidemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Phenotype I is an abnormality of chylomicrons and will result in triglycerides greater than 99 percentiles. Phenotype IIa consists mainly of LDL cholesterol abnormality and will have a total cholesterol concentration greater than 90 percentile and possibly apolipoprotein B greater than 90 percentile. Phenotype IIb consists of abnormality in LDL and VLDL cholesterol. This type will result in total cholesterol or triglycerides greater than the 90 percentile and apolipoprotein greater than the 90 percentile. Phenotype III is an abnormality in VLDL remnants and chylomicrons, which results in elevated total cholesterol and triglycerides greater than 90 percentile. Phenotype IV is mainly when VLDL is abnormal and results in total cholesterol greater than 90 percentile. This type can also present with triglycerides greater than 90 percentile and low HDL. Phenotype V is when chylomicrons and VLDL are abnormal and triglycerides are greater than 99 percentiles. [6] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Very Low Density Lipoprotein -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The impact of VLDL on cardiovascular diseases is mainly correlated to its role in atherosclerosis and coronary disease. [26] The accumulation of triglyceride-predominant lipoproteins such as chylomicrons and VLDL strongly contributes to the rupture of atherosclerotic plaques. Additionally, VLDL has been associated with peripheral artery disease, leading to occlusive limb events, vascular stiffness, and carotid artery thickness. [27] Besides the lipidic components of VLDL, its apolipoprotein composition also directly affects the development of cardiovascular disorders. Studies have shown that higher Apo-B and lower Apo C-III concentrations are associated with higher cardiovascular risk. [28] [29] This leads to the hypothesis that Apo-B is one of the major drivers in the development of atherosclerosis. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Cholesterol -- Cellular Level
retrieved_docs.content: Since cholesterol is mostly a lipophilic molecule, it does not dissolve well in the blood. For this reason, it is packaged in lipoproteins that have phospholipid and apolipoprotein. [3] Lipoproteins are made up of a lipid core (which can contain cholesterol esters and triglycerides) and a hydrophilic outer membrane comprising phospholipid, apolipoprotein, and free cholesterol. This allows the lipid molecules to move around the body through the blood and be transported to cells that need them. There are several types of lipoproteins that travel through the blood, and they each have different purposes. There are high-density lipoproteins (HDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL). Notably, LDL particles are thought to act as a major transporter of cholesterol; at least two-thirds of circulating cholesterol resides in LDL to the peripheral tissues. Conversely, HDL molecules are thought to do the opposite. They take excess cholesterol and return it to the liver for excretion. Clinically, these two lipoproteins are significant since high LDL and low HDL increase patients' risk of atherosclerotic vascular diseases. [4] [5] See Figure. The Molecular Structure of Cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Intermediate Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Lipoprotein metabolism exhibits heterogeneity within each lipoprotein fraction, with multiple parallel biochemical pathways contributing to the formation of distinct lipoprotein subfractions, each with unique metabolic properties. For instance, VLDL consists of 2 subfractions, VLDL1 and VLDL2, that may be separated by ultracentrifugation. VLDL1, or large VLDL particles, primarily transport triglycerides and are implicated in insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome. [16] In contrast, VLDL2, or small VLDL particles, are cholesterol-rich and contribute to variations in LDL cholesterol levels. Elevated VLDL2 levels are observed in familial hypercholesterolemia. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Cholesterol Levels -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Lipids circulate in the blood as lipoproteins and are composed of unesterified cholesterol, triglycerides, phospholipids, and proteins. The 5 major types of lipoproteins in the blood include chylomicrons, very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoprotein (IDL), low-density lipoprotein (LDL), and high-density lipoprotein (HDL). Each class of lipoprotein has a role in transporting cholesterol and triglycerides to specific destinations in the body.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dyslipidemia -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cholesterol and TG are the two most relevant plasma lipids. Because lipids are insoluble in water, they are transported in the blood with the help of lipoproteins. They constitute the hydrophobic core of lipoproteins separated from plasma by phospholipoproteins and apolipoproteins. Lipoproteins are classified into chylomicrons (CM), very low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoprotein (HDL). Plasma lipoproteins are subclassified based on their size, density, and composition, as listed in the table. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, High Density Lipoprotein -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The CETP pathway involves the transfer of lipids between HDL and other lipoproteins. For instance, CETP exchanges the cholesterol esters in the core of HDL particles to Apo-B-containing particles, VLDL, and LDL in exchange for triglycerides. The cholesterol, now contained in VLDL and LDL, can undergo uptake by the LDL receptor, resulting in the return of cholesterol from peripheral tissues to the liver. This exchange also enriches HDL in triglycerides and depletes the cholesterol ester core. As a result, hepatic lipases can metabolize the triglyceride-rich HDL, resulting in small HDL particles. These small HDL particles release their Apo-AI. The liver can then degrade Apo-A1 through an unknown mechanism, or the kidney can clear the free Apo-A1. It is also possible that HDL particles contain Apo-E, which allows HDL particles to undergo uptake by the LDL receptors into the liver and subsequently degrade. Endothelial cell lipase also metabolizes Apo-AI, leading to its clearance from circulation. In brief, the CETP pathway results in both the transfer of cholesterol in HDL to the liver and the elimination of HDL from the circulation through the degradation of Apo-AI. [6] [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Lipids -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: The interaction between water-fearing and fat-loving displays more clearly during lipid transport in plasma. Both cholesterol and triglycerides are nonpolar lipid molecules. Therefore, they must travel in the polar plasma with the help of lipoprotein particles. The main goal of lipoprotein is to help transport lipids (hydrophobic) in water. The structure of lipoprotein consists of triglycerides, cholesterol, phospholipids, and apolipoproteins. Apolipoproteins mainly function as carrier proteins but also serve as cofactors for enzymes that metabolize lipoproteins and help in lipid component exchange among lipoproteins. Some examples of lipoproteins include chylomicrons, very-low-density lipoproteins (VLDL), intermediate-density lipoproteins (IDL), low-density lipoproteins (LDL), and high-density lipoproteins (HDL). Each one is used in a different phase of lipid transport. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1880
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism constitute a broad category of metabolic conditions affecting the processing and regulation of fats in the body, and hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) is a specific disorder within this category. Hyperlipoproteinemia is characterized by abnormally high levels of lipoproteins in the blood, reflecting a particular manifestation of the underlying disturbances in lipid metabolism. Recognizing hyperlipoproteinemia as a subset of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores its role in the spectrum of lipid‑related diseases and informs targeted diagnostic and therapeutic approaches.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1941
retrieved_docs.content: Above reference range is used as an interpretation when evaluating disorders of lipid metabolism, indicating that laboratory values exceeding the normal limits may reflect underlying lipid metabolic abnormalities. Within the category of disorders of lipid metabolism, فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia) is recognized as a specific subtype, representing conditions characterized by excessive circulating lipoproteins. Additionally, ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) (elevated blood glucose/new‑onset prediabetes) is considered a member of the broader disorders of lipid metabolism, highlighting the clinical interrelationship between dysglycemia and lipid metabolic disturbances. Understanding these connections assists clinicians in interpreting abnormal lipid panels, identifying hyperlipoproteinemic states, and recognizing the metabolic overlap with early glucose dysregulation for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2538
retrieved_docs.content: Inclisiran and evolocumab are both classified as members of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “Other lipid modifying agents.” This classification indicates that each drug is used to modify lipid concentrations, particularly low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol, and is employed in the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and the reduction of cardiovascular risk. By belonging to the same ATC category, inclisiran and evolocumab share a therapeutic purpose of lowering lipid levels, although they may differ in their specific mechanisms of action and dosing regimens. Recognizing them as part of the “Other lipid modifying agents” group helps clinicians understand their role within the broader spectrum of lipid‑lowering therapies and supports informed decision‑making in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic diseases that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. In addition to lipid abnormalities, this category is linked to elevated blood glucose conditions such as new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)), highlighting the close interplay between lipid and glucose metabolic pathways. Recognizing that hyperlipoproteinemia and early diabetes can coexist within the spectrum of disorders of lipid metabolism underscores the importance of integrated assessment and management of both lipid and glucose abnormalities in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic conditions that include hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية) as a specific type of lipid disorder. Within this category, elevated blood glucose and new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ)) are also considered members, highlighting the clinical overlap between dyslipidemia and early glucose dysregulation. The disorder group is mapped to familial hyperlipidemia type IIa (فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ), a well‑characterized lipid abnormality that follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, as indicated by the relationship between Autosomal dominant inheritance and فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ. This interconnected framework underscores how lipid metabolism disorders, specific hyperlipoproteinemia subtypes, and early diabetic manifestations are linked both pathophysiologically and genetically, informing comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1460
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate and fenofibrate are both classified as fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As members of the fibrate class, these agents are used to alter lipid metabolism, typically to lower triglyceride levels and raise high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol, thereby contributing to the management of dyslipidaemia. Their shared classification underscores their similar pharmacologic actions and therapeutic role in cardiovascular risk reduction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 4 -- Community 2741
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a class of lipid‑modifying agents that are used to improve abnormal blood lipid profiles, particularly by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol. The primary members of this plain fibrate class are bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát. All three agents act as agonists of peroxisome proliferator‑activated receptor‑α (PPAR‑α), leading to increased oxidation of fatty acids, reduced hepatic production of very‑low‑density lipoprotein, and enhanced clearance of triglyceride‑rich lipoproteins. Because of these mechanisms, bezafibrát, klofibrát, and fenofibrát are employed in the management of hypertriglyceridaemia, mixed dyslipidaemia, and certain cases of secondary dyslipidaemia, contributing to cardiovascular risk reduction when used appropriately in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1908
retrieved_docs.content: Klofibrát, a fibrate‑type lipid‑lowering medication, produces a physiologic effect characterized by decreased lipolysis, reflecting its influence on the breakdown of triglycerides and overall lipid metabolism.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2462
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid modifying drugs constitute a therapeutic class of statin medications that lower cholesterol by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme in the hepatic cholesterol synthesis pathway. Atorvastatin, fluvastatin, and pravastatin are individual agents that belong to this class. By reducing low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein (HDL), these drugs are employed to manage hyperlipidemia and to lower the risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their shared mechanism of action and lipid‑modifying effects make them central components of preventive cardiology and patient care strategies aimed at controlling dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 2181
retrieved_docs.content: Fenofibrát is classified as a member of the “Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain” group, indicating that it belongs to the fibrate class of plain lipid‑modifying agents. As a fibrate, fenofibrát shares the typical pharmacologic actions of this drug family, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 127
retrieved_docs.content: Fibrates, lipid modifying drugs, plain constitute a therapeutic class of lipid‑lowering agents that includes the individual drugs bezafibrát, klofibrát, fenofibrát and gemfibrozil. Each of these agents is classified as a member of the fibrate group, reflecting their shared mechanism of reducing plasma triglycerides and modulating lipoprotein metabolism. Among them, klofibrát exerts a physiologic effect characterized by decreased lipolysis, which contributes to its lipid‑lowering action. However, klofibrát is contraindicated in the presence of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي (hepatic congestion), indicating that patients with this hepatic condition should avoid its use. Gemfibrozil is further linked to the formulation category G [Preparations], of which it is a parent compound, underscoring its inclusion in a specific preparation subgroup. Collectively, these relationships delineate the classification, pharmacologic effects, and safety considerations pertinent to fibrate therapy in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2780
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism are a specific category of metabolic disorder (اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي) and are also classified as hyperlipoproteinemia (فرط البروتينات الشحمية). These conditions involve abnormalities in the synthesis, transport, or breakdown of lipids, leading to elevated blood lipid levels and associated risks such as atherosclerosis and cardiovascular disease. Recognizing disorders of lipid metabolism as both a metabolic disturbance and a form of hyperlipoproteinemia underscores their role in systemic metabolic health and guides targeted diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1282
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin and pravastatin are both classified as members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels in patients. As members of the same therapeutic category, fluvastatin and pravastatin share a common mechanism of action and are used to manage hyperlipidemia and reduce cardiovascular risk. Recognizing that both agents belong to the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor, plain lipid‑modifying drug class helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual patient factors while understanding their shared pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1978
retrieved_docs.content: Bezafibrate is classified as a member of the fibrates, a group of plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As part of this drug class, bezafibrate shares the typical pharmacologic actions of fibrates, which are used to alter lipid profiles by lowering triglyceride levels and modestly increasing high‑density lipoprotein cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1085
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This drug class works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and decreased risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. As plain lipid‑modifying agents, these statins are commonly prescribed for the treatment of hyperlipidaemia and for primary and secondary prevention of cardiovascular events. While they share the same mechanism of action, each agent differs in potency, pharmacokinetic properties and individual safety profiles, allowing clinicians to tailor therapy to the specific needs of each patient.
